MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION
Off Calendar Oct-10-2024 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. PARHAM GOLESTANIAN, Esq. of Law Office of Allan A. Villanueva (tel: 925-365-1680) for Plaintiff Francisco Quilala.
Atty. STEPHEN MAZZARA, Esq. of Goldberg Segalla LLP (tel: 213-415-7200) for Defendants.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT SECURITAS SECURITY SERVICES USA., INC., REYNALDO DE LA CRUZ, AND LUIS CASTRO's MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendants' motion to compel arbitration and stay is denied.

Plaintiff pleads a claim for sexual harassment and therefore the action is not arbitrable under the federal Ending Forced Arbitration of Sexual Assault and Sexual Harassment Act ("EFAA").

Defendants convincingly demonstrate that the Federal Arbitration Act ("FAA") applies to this dispute. First, the parties' agreement specifically states "[t]he Agreement is governed by the Federal Arbitration Act." (Gomez Dec., Ex. A.) "[W]hen an agreement provides that its 'enforcement' shall be governed by the FAA, the FAA governs a party's motion to compel arbitration." (Victrola 89, LLC v. Jaman Properties 8 LLC (2020) 46 Cal.App.5th 337, 346.) Second, defendant Securitas is a nationwide security organization with branches in every state that serves all types of California and non-California residents. (Gomez Decl., par. 2.)

In 2022, Congress amended the FAA by passing the EFAA to render arbitration agreements unenforceable in sexual assault and sexual harassment cases that arise or accrue on or after March 2, 2022. (Doe v. Second Street Corp., 2024 WL 4350420 at *1.) The EFAA voids a pre-dispute arbitration agreement "with respect to a case which . . .relates to . . . the sexual harassment dispute." (9 U.S.C. 402(a) (emphasis added).)

Here, plaintiff pleads a cognizable claim for sexual harassment. The court liberally construes plaintiff's complaint per CCP 452. In 2019, the California Legislature clarified harassment law and noted "[a] single incident of harassing conduct is sufficient to create a triable issue regarding the existence of a hostile work environment if the harassing conduct has unreasonably interfered with the plaintiff's work performance or created an intimidating, hostile, or offensive work environment." (Government Code section 12923(b); see also Beltran v. Hard Rock Hotel Licensing, Inc. (2023) 97 Cal.App.5th 865, 878-880 [noting less demanding standard and inapplicability of former cases]; CACI 2524 [defining severe or pervasive]; Bailey v. San Francisco District Attorney's Office (2024) 16 Cal.5th 611 [use of single racial epithet could constitute severe conduct to support harassment claim].)

Plaintiff alleges several specific instances of objectionable conduct that occurred after the effective date of the EFAA. (Complaint, pars. 11-16.) Based on this claim, the case is exempt from arbitration. (See Turner v. Tesla, Inc. (N.D. Cal. 2023) 686 F.Supp.3d 917, 925 ["[T]he text of section 402(a) makes clear that its invalidation of an arbitration agreement extends to the entirety of the case"]; Doe, 2024 WL 4350420 at *10-13.) Accordingly, the court denies this motion.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Court reporter: Krishanna Derita, CSR# 11945 (tel: 925-360-6891 / email: krishkurt@aol.com).
